quests.title: The Descent of the Ma'idah (The Table Spread)
quests.description: The disciples of Isa, peace be upon him, requested a sign from Allah in the form of a table laden with food from the heavens. Isa prayed to Allah, and the Ma'idah descended as a testament to Allah's power and to strengthen the faith of the believers.
quests.outcome: The table descended, filled with food, and served as a sign of Allah's grace and power, reinforcing the disciples' faith.

quests.title: The Clay Birds
quests.description: As a young prophet, Isa, peace be upon him, fashioned birds from clay. Through Allah's permission, he breathed life into them, and they flew away, demonstrating his divine gift and establishing his prophethood.
quests.outcome: The clay birds came to life and flew away, solidifying Isa's status as a prophet of Allah and a recipient of divine miracles.

quests.title: Healing the Leper
quests.description: Isa, peace be upon him, was granted the power to heal the sick and afflicted. He encountered a leper, ostracized and suffering, and through Allah's will, Isa touched him and healed him completely of his disease.
quests.outcome: The leper was miraculously healed, becoming a testament to Isa's divine power and the compassion he showed towards the marginalized.

quests.title: Raising the Dead
quests.description: With Allah's permission, Isa, peace be upon him, was given the ability to bring the dead back to life. He resurrected a deceased individual, restoring them to their loved ones and further solidifying his prophethood.
quests.outcome: The deceased was brought back to life, serving as a powerful sign of Allah's power and Isa's role as a messenger of God.

feats.title: The Virgin Birth
feats.description: Isa, peace be upon him, was born to Maryam (Mary) without a father, a miraculous event ordained by Allah. This unique birth signifies his special status as a prophet and messenger of God.
feats.significance: It demonstrates Allah's power to create life in extraordinary ways and highlights the purity and piety of Maryam.

feats.title: Speaking in the Cradle
feats.description: As an infant, Isa, peace be upon him, spoke from the cradle to defend his mother's honor against accusations of impropriety. He declared his prophethood and affirmed his role as a servant of Allah.
feats.significance: This miraculous speech validated Maryam's innocence and served as an early sign of Isa's divinely appointed mission.

feats.title: Ascension to Heaven
feats.description: According to Islamic belief, Isa, peace be upon him, was not crucified but was instead raised up to heaven by Allah. He will return to Earth before the Day of Judgment to establish justice and defeat the Dajjal (Antichrist).
feats.significance: This ascension signifies Allah's protection of his prophet and highlights Isa's future role in the events leading up to the Day of Judgment.

companions.name: Maryam (Mary)
companions.description: As Isa's mother, Maryam is a constant companion and supporter. She is revered for her piety and faith, and played a vital role in fulfilling God's plan.

companions.name: Hawariyyun (Disciples)
companions.description: The Hawariyyun were the disciples of Isa, who believed in him and supported his mission. They are considered righteous individuals who followed Isa's teachings.

companions.name: The Table Spread (Ma'idah)
companions.description: The 'Table Spread' refers to the miraculous provision of food from heaven requested by the disciples as a sign of Isa's divine power. It served as a tangible demonstration of God's support for Isa and his followers.

companions.name: Gabriel (Jibril)
companions.description: Gabriel, a high-ranking angel, served as a messenger between God and Isa. He conveyed divine revelations and guidance to Isa throughout his life and ministry.

name: Isa (Jesus)

batch5_migration.htmlRawContent:
📜 Jibreel (Gabriel)

Home → Islamic → Divine Beings → Jibreel
📜

Jibreel (جبريل)

The Angel of Revelation - Ruh al-Qudus (The Holy Spirit)

The greatest and most honored of all angels, entrusted with delivering divine revelation from Allah to His prophets. Jibreel is the intermediary between the Divine and humanity, the bringer of guidance and spiritual strength.

Note on Representation: Islamic tradition forbids visual depiction of angels. This page focuses on theological understanding from Quranic and Hadith sources.

Attributes & Nature

Nature
Created from Light (Nur)
Primary Role
Bearer of Divine Revelation (Wahy)
Rank
Chief of Angels (Sayyid al-Mala'ika)
Wings
600 Wings (as seen by Muhammad)
Other Names
Ruh al-Qudus (Holy Spirit), Ruh al-Amin (Trustworthy Spirit)
Presence
Appears in Various Forms

The Nature of Jibreel

Jibreel is the greatest of all angels, standing closest to Allah's throne. Created from pure light, he possesses no free will and exists solely to execute Allah's commands. His primary function is delivering divine revelation, but he also serves as a source of spiritual strength and guidance.

Physical Description

The Prophet Muhammad saw Jibreel in his true angelic form only twice. The angel's magnificence was so overwhelming that his 600 wings filled the horizon from east to west. Each wing was adorned with pearls and rubies. However, Jibreel typically appeared to prophets in human form, most commonly as a man named Dihya al-Kalbi, known for his extraordinary beauty.

The True Form:
"Indeed, he [Muhammad] saw him [Jibreel] in another descent at the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary, near it is the Garden of Refuge, when there covered the Lote Tree that which covered it. The sight [of the Prophet] did not swerve, nor did it transgress its limit. He certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord." - Quran 53:13-18

Key Roles & Stories

1. The First Revelation

Jibreel appeared to Muhammad in the Cave of Hira in 610 CE, commanding him to "Read!" (Iqra!). This was the beginning of the Quranic revelation that would continue for 23 years. Jibreel would visit periodically to deliver verses, and Muhammad would memorize and recite them to his companions.

2. The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj)

Jibreel accompanied the Prophet Muhammad on the miraculous night journey from Makkah to Jerusalem (Isra) and then through the seven heavens to the Divine Presence (Mi'raj). During this journey, the five daily prayers were ordained. Jibreel served as guide, protector, and witness to this extraordinary spiritual experience.

3. Appearances to Other Prophets

  • To Maryam (Mary): Announced the miraculous birth of Isa (Jesus)
  • To Ibrahim (Abraham): Brought news of a son (Isaac) and rescued him from fire
  • To Lut (Lot): Warned of impending destruction and guided his escape
  • To Musa (Moses): Assisted at Mount Sinai and throughout his mission
  • To All Prophets: Served as the consistent messenger of divine guidance

4. The Hadith of Jibreel

In one of the most famous hadith, Jibreel appeared to the Prophet's companions in the form of a man dressed in pure white with jet-black hair, showing no signs of travel. He questioned Muhammad about Islam, Iman (faith), and Ihsan (excellence), effectively teaching the fundamental structure of the religion. After he departed, the Prophet revealed: "That was Jibreel; he came to teach you your religion."

Teaching Through Questions:
Jibreel's method demonstrates angelic pedagogy: appearing as a humble student to teach profound lessons. He asked perfect questions to elicit complete answers for the benefit of all listeners. - Sahih Muslim, Book 1, Hadith 1

Jibreel's Functions

Primary Function
Delivering Revelation (Wahy)
Secondary Role
Providing Spiritual Strength (Taqwiyah)
Guardian Role
Protecting Prophets During Missions
Teaching Role
Instructing Prophets in Religious Knowledge
Warfare Role
Leading Angelic Armies in Battle of Badr
Annual Role
Reviewing Quran with Prophet Every Ramadan

Relationship with Muhammad

Jibreel's relationship with the Prophet Muhammad was unique in scope and intimacy. For 23 years, he was Muhammad's constant spiritual companion, teacher, and friend. The angel would appear sometimes daily, sometimes weekly, delivering revelation, answering questions, and providing guidance on complex matters.

Notable Interactions:

  • Regular Visits: Jibreel descended to deliver Quranic verses as events required guidance
  • Annual Review: Every Ramadan, Jibreel reviewed the entire revealed Quran with Muhammad
  • Final Review: In Muhammad's final Ramadan, Jibreel reviewed the Quran twice, signaling the Prophet's impending death
  • Comfort in Difficulty: During the "Year of Sorrow," Jibreel provided spiritual consolation
  • Military Assistance: Appeared at the Battle of Badr leading 1,000 angels to aid the Muslims

Theological Significance

The Chain of Revelation

Jibreel represents the intermediary link in the chain of divine communication: Allah → Jibreel → Prophet → Humanity

This chain emphasizes that prophets do not access divine knowledge directly but receive it through angelic mediation, maintaining the transcendence of Allah while enabling human guidance.

Belief in Jibreel as Article of Faith

Belief in angels, particularly Jibreel, is one of the six pillars of Islamic faith (Iman). The Quran explicitly states that denial of Jibreel is equivalent to disbelief:

"Say: 'Whoever is an enemy to Jibreel—for he brings down the revelation to your heart by Allah's will, confirming what came before it and as guidance and glad tidings for the believers— whoever is an enemy to Allah, His angels, His messengers, Jibreel, and Mikail, then indeed Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers.'" - Quran 2:97-98

Jibreel in Sufi Mysticism

Sufi tradition views Jibreel as the archetype of the spiritual guide and the embodiment of active intellect. He represents the bridge between divine knowledge and human understanding. Some Sufis interpret the appearance of Jibreel as symbolic of the awakening of spiritual consciousness within the seeker.

Symbolic Meanings:

  • Divine Inspiration: Sudden insights and spiritual revelations
  • Active Intellect: The faculty through which divine knowledge enters human consciousness
  • Spiritual Strength: Inner fortitude to maintain faith during trials
  • Perfect Servanthood: Complete obedience and submission to divine will

🎭 Archetypal Patterns

🔗 Related Concepts

Within Islamic Tradition

  • Allah - The One who sends Jibreel
  • Muhammad - Final recipient of Jibreel's revelation
  • Other Angels - Mikail, Israfil, Azrael
  • Creation - Angels created from light
  • Quran - The revelation brought by Jibreel

Similar Beings in Other Traditions

  • Gabriel (Jewish) - Same angel, different tradition
  • Gabriel (Christian) - Announced Jesus's birth
  • Metatron (Jewish) - Heavenly scribe and messenger
  • Holy Spirit (Christian) - Sometimes equated in theology
  • Sraosha (Zoroastrian) - Divine messenger

    • Sources

    • Quran - Surah 2:97-98, 53:1-18, 66:4, 81:19-21
    • Sahih Bukhari - Book on the Beginning of Creation, Hadith on Angels
    • Sahih Muslim - Book 1, Hadith 1 (Hadith of Jibreel)
    • Tafsir Ibn Kathir - Commentary on angelic verses
    • Al-Ghazali - "The Revival of Religious Sciences" on angelic nature
    • Ibn Arabi - "The Meccan Revelations" on spiritual interpretation of angels

Jibreel (Gabriel) - Islamic Theology
World Mythos Home | Islamic Tradition | Browse All Divine Beings

deeds: Return to Islamic Tradition Main Page
description:
Islam recognizes 25 prophets by name in the Quran, though many more were sent throughout history.
                All prophets brought the same core message: worship Allah alone (Tawhid). They are models of righteousness,
                patience, and submission to the Divine will.

Title: Khalilullah (Friend of Allah)

Father of monotheism, builder of the Kaaba, willing to sacrifice his son for Allah.
                    Patriarch of all three Abrahamic faiths.
icon:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 64 64">
  <circle cx="32" cy="32" r="28.8" fill="#6a5acd" opacity="0.2"/>
  <rect x="19.2" y="16" width="25.6" height="32" rx="4" fill="#6a5acd"/>

keyMyths.title: The Virgin Birth
keyMyths.description: Isa's miraculous birth to Maryam (Mary) without a father is a sign of Allah's power and a test of faith. It highlights Maryam's piety and purity, and Isa's divine mission from the very beginning.

keyMyths.title: Speaking in the Cradle
keyMyths.description: As an infant, Isa miraculously spoke to defend his mother's honor against accusations of impropriety. This confirmed his prophethood and silenced his detractors, demonstrating his early divine wisdom.

keyMyths.title: Performing Miracles
keyMyths.description: Isa was granted the power to perform miracles by Allah, including healing the sick, restoring sight to the blind, and raising the dead. These miracles served as signs of his prophethood and proofs of Allah's power.

keyMyths.title: The Last Supper Narrative
keyMyths.description: In Islamic tradition, the last supper is interpreted as a farewell gathering of Isa with his disciples, where he forewarns them of impending trials and the importance of remaining steadfast in their faith before his ascension.

keyMyths.title: The Ascension to Heaven
keyMyths.description: Rather than being crucified, Isa was raised up to Allah, escaping the plots of his enemies. This ascension signifies his honor and protection by Allah, and the promise of his return before the Day of Judgment.

achievements: Born of a virgin (Maryam) without a father, a sign of Allah's power.
Spoke in the cradle to defend his mother's honor.
Performed miracles, including healing the sick and raising the dead.
Received divine revelation (the Injil/Gospel) from Allah.
Ascended to Heaven without experiencing death.
Will return to Earth before the Day of Judgment to establish justice.
Confirmed the Torah revealed to Musa (Moses)

sources.name: The Holy Quran
sources.description: The central religious text of Islam, containing accounts of Isa's life and teachings.
sources.title: The Holy Quran

sources.name: Tafsir al-Tabari
sources.description: A classical Sunni Quranic commentary providing interpretations of verses related to Isa.
sources.title: Tafsir al-Tabari

sources.name: Ibn Kathir's Tafsir
sources.description: Another prominent Sunni Quranic commentary offering detailed explanations of Isa's role in Islam.
sources.title: Ibn Kathir's Tafsir

sources.name: Sahih al-Bukhari
sources.description: A collection of hadith (sayings and actions of the Prophet Muhammad) that provide context and supplementary information about Isa.
sources.title: Sahih al-Bukhari

sources.name: Sahih Muslim
sources.description: Another highly regarded collection of hadith that mentions Isa and his significance in Islamic eschatology.
sources.title: Sahih Muslim

worshipPractices.name: Reverence as a Prophet
worshipPractices.description: Muslims revere Isa as a prophet of God, one of the most important figures in Islamic tradition. They do not worship him as divine, but hold him in high esteem for his piety, miracles, and message of submission to Allah. Prayers are offered for his well-being and blessings, alongside other prophets.

worshipPractices.name: Following Ethical Teachings
worshipPractices.description: Muslims strive to emulate Isa's ethical and moral teachings as outlined in the Quran. This includes practicing compassion, forgiveness, and helping the poor and needy. These actions are seen as acts of worship by demonstrating obedience to God's commands as delivered through His prophets.

festivals.name: Mawlid an-Nabi (Prophet's Birthday)
festivals.description: While primarily focused on Muhammad's birth, the celebrations often include remembrance and appreciation of all prophets, including Isa. Stories of Isa's life and miracles may be shared, emphasizing his role as a messenger of God.

cultural.modernLegacy: Isa (Jesus) continues to be a prominent figure in interfaith dialogue between Muslims and Christians, fostering mutual respect and understanding. His story, as recounted in the Quran, inspires artistic and literary works, often highlighting themes of peace, compassion, and divine guidance. His narratives are also commonly used in Islamic sermons and teachings to reinforce core Islamic values.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Asclepius, like Isa, is associated with healing and miraculous abilities. Both figures are revered for their power to cure the sick and restore health.

Bodhisattvas, especially those who vow to liberate all beings, share a similar characteristic with Isa in their commitment to helping humanity and guiding them towards spiritual enlightenment. Both are seen as compassionate figures dedicated to the well-being of others.

Avataras, particularly those of Vishnu, are divine incarnations sent to Earth to restore Dharma (righteousness) and guide humanity. This aligns with the Islamic view of Isa as a prophet sent by God to guide people towards the right path.
The Saoshyant is a future savior figure who will renew the world and bring about a final judgment. This role is similar to Islamic beliefs about Isa's return before the Day of Judgement.

associations.name: Compassion
associations.significance: Isa (Jesus) is revered for his miracles of healing and his teachings emphasizing mercy and love.

associations.name: Star
associations.significance: Stars symbolize guidance, and Isa (Jesus) is seen as a guide to righteousness and submission to God.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: While Islam rejects the Trinity, the number three can represent the concept of divine intervention and the miraculous nature of Isa's (Jesus') birth.

associations.name: Olive Branch
associations.significance: The olive branch symbolizes peace, reflecting Isa's (Jesus') role as a messenger of divine peace and reconciliation.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White represents purity and innocence, reflecting Isa's (Jesus') sinless nature and spiritual purity in Islamic belief.

associations.name: Miracle
associations.significance: Isa (Jesus) is strongly associated with performing miracles, signifying divine power and favor.

associations.name: Dove
associations.significance: The dove is a symbol of peace and divine spirit, representing the holy spirit associated with Isa (Jesus).

extendedContent.title: Early Life & Miraculous Birth
extendedContent.content:
In Islamic tradition, Isa (Jesus) is revered as a prophet of God, born to Maryam (Mary), a pious and chaste woman chosen by Allah. The Quran (3:45-47) recounts the annunciation of Jesus's birth by angels, who inform Maryam that she will conceive a son, a 'Word from Allah,' who will be held in honor in this world and the Hereafter. This miraculous conception, without a male father, is a testament to Allah's power and is explicitly stated in the Quran (19:20-21). Maryam's unwavering faith and submission to Allah's will are emphasized, highlighting her role as a virtuous and chosen woman. The circumstances of Jesus's birth emphasize his special status as a prophet sent directly by God, distinct from the conventional understanding of human procreation. The Quranic narrative avoids the Christian concept of the Trinity and emphasizes Jesus's role as a servant and messenger of Allah.

Furthermore, Islamic sources detail the hardships Maryam faced due to her unmarried pregnancy. The Quran (19:22-26) describes her retreat to a remote place during childbirth and her distress at facing societal judgment. In this challenging moment, a voice from beneath her (interpreted as either Jesus himself or an angel) comforts her and directs her to a spring and a date palm, providing sustenance. This episode underscores the divine support granted to Maryam and her son, reinforcing their blessed status. The emphasis on Maryam's trials and tribulations also serves as a reminder of the importance of faith and resilience in the face of adversity.

Moreover, Jesus is described as speaking from the cradle, proclaiming his prophethood and his mission to establish prayer and charity (Quran 19:30-33). This miraculous utterance, occurring shortly after his birth, serves as undeniable proof of his divine appointment and sets him apart from ordinary children. This early proclamation of his prophetic mission underscores the Islamic belief that Jesus was a prophet from the very beginning, chosen by Allah to guide humanity to the right path.

extendedContent.title: Teachings, Miracles, and Prophetic Mission
extendedContent.content:
Isa's teachings, as presented in the Quran, center on the oneness of God (Tawhid) and the importance of submission to Allah's will. He called upon the Children of Israel to worship Allah alone and to uphold the laws of the Torah, while also clarifying and reforming certain interpretations. The Quran (5:46) states that Jesus confirmed the Torah and brought the Gospel (Injil), which contained guidance and light. However, Muslims believe that the original Gospel has been altered over time, and therefore, the Quran serves as the final and complete revelation. Jesus is portrayed as a compassionate and merciful prophet, dedicated to guiding humanity towards righteousness and salvation.

Islamic tradition attributes numerous miracles to Isa, demonstrating his divine power and validating his prophetic claims. The Quran (3:49) mentions that Jesus healed the blind and the lepers, raised the dead, and fashioned a bird from clay, breathed life into it, and it became a real bird by the permission of Allah. These miracles are seen as signs of Allah's favor upon Jesus and evidence of his prophetic status. They are not interpreted as signs of divinity but rather as divine gifts bestowed upon a chosen messenger. The emphasis remains on Allah's ultimate power and control over all things.

Isa's prophetic mission faced resistance from some of the Children of Israel, who rejected his message and plotted against him. The Quran (3:54) mentions that the disbelievers conspired to kill him, but Allah thwarted their plans. This highlights the theme of divine protection afforded to prophets and the ultimate triumph of good over evil. The resistance faced by Jesus underscores the challenges faced by prophets throughout history in their mission to guide humanity towards the truth.

extendedContent.title: The Last Supper and the Divine Rescue
extendedContent.content:
The Islamic perspective on the events leading up to Jesus's purported crucifixion differs significantly from the Christian narrative. The Quran (4:157-158) explicitly states that the Jews did not kill or crucify Jesus, but it was made to appear so to them. This verse is interpreted in various ways by Islamic scholars. Some believe that Allah created a likeness of Jesus and placed it on another person, who was then crucified in his place. Others suggest that Jesus was raised to heaven before he could be harmed. Regardless of the specific interpretation, the core belief is that Allah rescued Jesus from his enemies and prevented him from being killed on the cross. This divine intervention reinforces the Islamic belief in Allah's power and protection of his prophets.

The Quran does not provide details about a 'Last Supper' in the same way as the Christian Gospels. However, some Islamic scholars suggest that there was a final gathering of Jesus with his disciples before his departure. The emphasis in Islamic tradition is not on the elements of bread and wine, but rather on the unity and loyalty of Jesus's followers. The focus remains on the message of Tawhid and the importance of adhering to Allah's commands. The absence of a detailed Last Supper narrative in the Quran underscores the differences in theological emphasis between Islam and Christianity.

The belief that Jesus was not crucified is a fundamental tenet of Islamic faith and distinguishes it from Christian theology. It reinforces the Islamic belief in the immutability and omnipotence of Allah, who would not allow his prophet to be humiliated and killed by his enemies. This difference in belief has significant implications for the understanding of salvation and the role of Jesus in Islamic theology.

extendedContent.title: Second Coming and Role in the End Times
extendedContent.content:
Islamic eschatology includes the belief in the second coming of Isa (Jesus) before the Day of Judgment. According to Islamic tradition, Jesus will descend from heaven near a white minaret in Damascus. He will come as a just ruler and a follower of the Prophet Muhammad, affirming the finality of Muhammad's prophethood and the validity of Islamic law. Jesus will break the cross, kill the pig (prohibiting practices deemed un-Islamic), and abolish the jizya (tax on non-Muslims under Islamic rule), signifying the universal acceptance of Islam. This event will mark a period of peace and justice on earth before the final tribulation.

During his second coming, Jesus will fight against the Dajjal (Antichrist), a figure of immense evil and deception who will mislead humanity. Jesus will ultimately defeat the Dajjal, leading to a triumph of good over evil. This event is seen as a crucial part of the end-times narrative and a fulfillment of prophecy. Jesus's role in defeating the Dajjal underscores his continued importance in Islamic eschatology and his role as a righteous warrior fighting for the cause of Allah.

After defeating the Dajjal, Jesus will live on earth for a period of time, ruling with justice and peace. He will eventually die and be buried alongside the Prophet Muhammad in Medina. His presence during the end times serves as a reminder of the importance of faith and righteousness in the face of adversity and a testament to the ultimate triumph of good over evil. The belief in Jesus's second coming is a significant aspect of Islamic eschatology and highlights his continued relevance in Islamic belief and practice.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Influence in Islamic Culture
extendedContent.content:
Isa (Jesus) holds a position of great respect and veneration in Islamic culture and piety. He is considered one of the most important prophets in Islam, mentioned frequently in the Quran and regarded as a righteous servant of Allah. His name is often invoked with reverence, and Muslims commonly say 'peace be upon him' (alayhi as-salam) after mentioning his name, a sign of respect reserved for prophets. This veneration is reflected in Islamic art, literature, and popular piety, where Jesus is often depicted as a compassionate and wise figure.

Maryam (Mary), the mother of Jesus, is also highly esteemed in Islam. She is considered one of the most virtuous women in Islamic history and is the only woman mentioned by name in the Quran. A chapter in the Quran (Surah Maryam) is dedicated to her story, highlighting her piety, chastity, and unwavering faith. Maryam's example serves as an inspiration for Muslim women, who admire her for her strength, resilience, and devotion to Allah. The reverence for Maryam underscores the importance of family and motherhood in Islamic culture.

The stories of Jesus and Maryam have inspired countless works of art, literature, and poetry throughout the Islamic world. Their narratives are often used to teach moral lessons and to promote values such as compassion, humility, and faith in Allah. Mosques and Islamic centers often feature depictions of biblical scenes, including the birth of Jesus and his miracles. The legacy of Jesus and Maryam continues to resonate deeply within Islamic culture, serving as a reminder of the shared heritage between Islam and Christianity and the importance of interfaith understanding and respect.

shortDescription: A prophet revered in Islam, believed to be the Messiah, born of the Virgin Mary, and who will return before the Day of Judgment.
subtitle: The Messiah, Prophet of God, Son of Mary
